data.title: Congress is taking stock of proposed ethics rules
data.description:
Last week the Congress came to life with palpitations over an ethics issue that did not exhibit the usual gotcha cross-fire of partisan ethics war accusations. Instead, it showed genuine concern about the survival of the institution given its all-time low level of public approval ratings. A new bipartisan congressional stock trading ban bill was introduced in the Senate last week as a companion to an identical House bill dropped on Sept. 3. A third bill was reported from a House committee last Wednesday.

The immediate problem is the perception that Congress’s members are insider-trading on the stock market and getting richer while the country grows poorer. Now, on the cusp of the midterm elections, there seems to be a renewed interest in forging some kind of nonpartisan solution that has credibility and real consequences.

The dial for action this time was pushed by the threat of House floor action. A pending discharge petition would permit a vote on a bill that bans not only new stock trading by members and their immediate families, but also requires them to divest all current stock holdings within 180 days.

All of the major ethics reforms of the past have succeeded because members realized that their reputations and that of Congress depended on agreeing to credible remedies that transcend the partisan divide.

In Federalist 57, James Madison wrote that the aim of a constitution should be to obtain rulers who have “the wisdom to discern…the common good of society,” and then “to take the most effective precautions for keeping them virtuous.” Republican virtue, as commonly understood in Madison’s time, meant putting the public good above personal and private interests.

Its protection and promotion is reflected in the Constitution’s separation of powers, checks and balances, and Bill of Rights — all designed to guard against autocratic rule. Rather than listing the virtues expected, the Constitution left it to each house to determine its rules of proceeding, punish its members for disorderly behavior, and, by a two-thirds vote, to expel them.

It wasn’t until the mid-20th century that Congress began to specify, by rules and statutes, the standards of official conduct to prevent the amassing of power and wealth by public officials. Until then, the rare instances of misconduct were handled on an ad hoc basis. It wasn’t until 1958 that Congress adopted the first “Code of Ethics for Government” that applied to all government officials and employees, including Congress. In 1964 the Senate created its first ethics committee. The House followed suit the following year.

In 1968 the House adopted its own code of official conduct and its first financial disclosure requirements, and directed its ethics committee to enforce all the new ethics rules. Since then, there have been major overhauls of House and Senate ethics rules at the end of each decade. In the 1978 Ethics in Government Act, full, annual financial disclosure statements were required by all high-level government officials, across the branches.

In the Ethics Reform Act in 1989, strict limits on earned outside incomes were imposed, along with a ban on paid honoraria in return for a member pay raise. (Disclosure: I was minority staff director on the 1989 bipartisan ethics task force under its co-chair, Rep. Lynn Martin (R-Ill.))

There are those who argue that the pending reform calling for members not only to stop trading in stocks but to divest themselves of all current holdings is not necessary because financial disclosure statements already publicly reveal what assets members own and point to any potential conflicts of interest. But those revelations alone do not reveal any intentional conflict of interest actions taken. 

In July 2025, Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) was the only Republican who supported a bill reported by the Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee to extend the stock ban to the next president and vice president. That measure has since languished on the Senate floor calendar. However, it was enough to provoke the ire of President Donald Trump who called Hawley a “second tier” senator. Numerous House Democrats still agree any stock trading ban should also apply not only to president and vice president, but to other high level executive branch officials.

The House Administration Committee last Wednesday, on a 7-to-4 party-line vote, reported a compromise bill to ban all future stock trading by members and employees, but would not require them to sell off their current holdings. Meantime, the discharge petition on the issue, while so far attracting only around 80 of the 218 signatures needed, could well take-off quickly if the Speaker does not soon schedule floor action that allows for some alternative amendments.

Don Wolfensberger is a 28-year congressional staff veteran culminating as chief of staff of the House Rules Committee in 1995. He is author of, “Congress and the People: Deliberative Democracy on Trial” (2008), and, “Changing Cultures in Congress: From Fair Play to Power Plays” (2018).

data.title: Live updates: Jack Smith defends Trump probes in first public testimony
data.description:
Former special counsel Jack Smith is testifying on Capitol Hill Thursday morning, in his first public testimony about his investigations into President Trump. 

Smith stood by his decision to probe Trump over efforts to overturn the 2020 election and his mishandling of classified documents

“No one should be above the law in our country, and the law required that [Trump] be held to account. So that is what I did,” Smith said in his opening remarks.

Trump is on his way back to the U.S. from Davos, Switzerland, where he met with world leaders and unveiled his new Board of Peace in Gaza at the annual World Economic Forum. 

No major European allies attended a Thursday signing ceremony for the peace board initiative, however, as tensions flare with NATO amid Trump’s bid to acquire Greenland

After he delivered remarks Wednesday, Trump announced that allies reached a “framework” of a deal on his quest to acquire Greenland. The president also said he would no longer impose tariffs on Denmark and other NATO countries, walking back threats to forcefully take over the island nation.

Later Thursday, the House will vote on its last batch of funding measures, including a Department of Homeland Security bill and another minibus appropriations package. The lower chamber will also weigh a war powers resolution seeking to prevent further military action in Venezuela.

Vice President Vance, meanwhile, will travel to Toledo, Ohio, and then Minneapolis, where protests have flared over federal immigration enforcement efforts across the state.

Follow live below.

data.title: House narrowly tees up final votes on last four funding bills
data.description:
The House on Thursday advanced its final four appropriations bills, including the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding bill, bringing the House one step closer to meeting the Jan. 30 funding deadline.

The lower chamber voted 214-213 on the rule, which sets up debate and two separate votes on final passage later in the afternoon. One vote will include measures to fund the departments of Defense, Transportation, Housing and Urban Development, Health and Human Services, Labor, Education and other related agencies. The House will separately vote on the DHS bill.

The rule vote briefly stalled on the floor as GOP leaders, contending with a razor-thin margin, tried to win over holdouts and flip two GOP “no” votes. House Majority Whip Tom Emmer (R-Minn.) huddled with Freedom Caucus Chair Andy Harris (R-Md.) — who had not yet voted — and Rep. Chip Roy (R-Texas) in the Speaker’s Lobby while Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) spoke with holdouts Rep. Andy Ogles (R-Tenn) and Rep. Zach Nunn (R-Iowa) on the House floor.

Reps. Lauren Boebert (R-Colo.) and Victoria Spartz (R-Ind.) flipped their “no” votes to “yes” and enough holdouts cast their votes.

The Department of Homeland Security bill would allow tens of billions of dollars to flow into Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). It would also provide funding for the Federal Emergency Management Agency, the Transportation Security Administration, the U.S. Secret Service, the Disaster Relief Fund and other programs and agencies. 

Internal Republican disputes had stalled the bills’ advancement through the House Rules Committee on Wednesday.

One major issue GOP leaders faced was requests from midwestern Republicans to authorize year-round sales of E15 ethanol, a fuel blend with 10.5 percent to 15 percent ethanol blended with gasoline that has historically been restricted in the summer due to concerns about smog. 

 GOP leaders ultimately struck a deal with holdouts to create a rural domestic energy council tasked with addressing the authorization of E15 ethanol. The council was established after the rule was adopted.

The House also passed an amendment to the rule that would repeal a provision included in a November funding measure allowing senators to earn a significant amount of money from litigation challenging the seizure of their phone records as part of former special counsel Jack Smith’s Jan. 6, 2021, Capitol attack probe. The senators could sue for $500,000 if their data was sought or obtained without their prior notification. 

The House will also be debating two other amendments to the package. One, sponsored by Rep. Ralph Norman (R-S.C.), would prohibit funding for “certain community funding projects.”

Another, sponsored by Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), would prohibit the use of funds to implement a section of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, “including any requirements enabling or supporting vehicle ‘kill switch’ technology.”

The minibus package and DHS bill are the product of fragile negotiations between bipartisan appropriators. House Appropriations Committee Chair Tom Cole (R-Okla.) and Rep. Rosa DeLauro (Conn.), the senior Democrat on the panel, have thrown their weight behind it.

The minibus package is expected to pass the House with broad bipartisan support. However, the DHS bill could be a tight vote as a majority of Democrats are likely to oppose it following the fatal shooting of a woman by an ICE agent in Minneapolis.

data.title: More state legislative chambers competitive for midterms than in past cycles: Election handicapper
data.description:
More state legislative chambers are competitive at this point in the midterms compared to the 2022 and 2024 cycles, according to new analysis from a top election handicapper. 

Sabato’s Crystal Ball, a product of the Center for Politics at the University of Virginia, found 15 state legislative chambers in competitive territory at this point in the 2026 cycle, compared to 12 at the same time in 2024 and 10 in 2022. 

The most recent cycle with a higher number of competitive state chambers at this point was 2018, when Democrats won big with a blue wave up and down ballot. 

This year, Sabato’s considers nine of the competitive chambers to be toss-ups, including the GOP-controlled legislatures in Arizona and Wisconsin, the Michigan House and the New Hampshire House — as well as the Democrat-held Senates in Michigan and Minnesota. The Minnesota House, which is currently tied, is also a toss-up. 

Republicans are playing defense in a total of eight competitive state chambers, while Democrats control four. 

“In many states, Democrats are looking forward to a favorable cycle, driven by a reaction to President Donald Trump and his policies. However, in some states, voters may be tired of Democratic governance at the state level, creating cross-cutting pressures,” Sabato’s columnist Louis Jacobson wrote in his analysis. 

Emboldened by a string of off-year wins, the Democratic Legislative Campaign Committee (DLCC) expanded its target map for state chambers last month, with aims of flipping eight Republican-majority chambers, creating 10 Democratic supermajorities and breaking 10 GOP supermajorities. 

Thousands of state legislative seats in 46 states are on the ballot in November.

data.title: Virginia Democrats schedule April 21 redistricting special election
data.description:
Virginia Democrats scheduled an April 21 special election for a ballot measure that would give lawmakers the ability to temporarily redraw a new House map as the party looks to do so ahead of the November midterms.

Democrats introduced legislation Wednesday that sets the schedule for a special election regarding the measure that would allow lawmakers the ability to temporarily redistrict mid-decade by October 2030 and bypass the state’s redistricting commission. Lawmakers passed the constitutional amendment twice — once before and after the 2025 elections.

The legislation introduced Tuesday also includes the ballot language for the initiative the party plans to put before voters.

If approved, the ballot language would state: “Should the Constitution of Virginia be amended to allow the General Assembly to temporarily adopt new congressional districts to restore fairness in the upcoming elections, while ensuring Virginia’s standard redistricting process resumes for all future redistricting after the 2030 census?”

The legislation needs to pass in both chambers and be signed by Gov. Abigail Spanberger (D) before the special election can be scheduled. The legislation is likely to easily pass in the state legislature given Democrats’ majority.

The move puts Democrats one step closer toward redrawing Old Dominion’s congressional lines before the midterms. Virginia Democrats, who enjoy a 6-5 edge in their congressional delegation, are eyeing either a 10-1 map or a 9-2 map, giving the party between three and four pickup opportunities in the lower chamber.

Democrats say that their proposed map will be finalized by the end of the month, so voters can see what kind of map lawmakers are looking to put into place.

In Florida, Republicans are also expected to convene in April for a special redistricting session, where the party believes it could net up to five new seats.

data.title: Grassley defends GOP incumbents as Trump wades into primary fights
data.description:
Sen. Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa) on Thursday defended several of his Senate GOP colleagues who are staring down potentially tricky reelection fights after President Trump indicated that he will not support them this year.

Trump over the weekend announced that he will back Rep. Julia Letlow’s (R-La.) primary bid to unseat Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.), a move that is counter to Senate Republican leaders who are supporting the two-term incumbent. 

In addition, the president is expected to stay neutral in the Texas Senate race where Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) is part of a three-way primary fight. 

“The president will have to do what he has to do. We’re going to do what we have to do to protect the integrity of the United States Senate Republican majority,” Grassley said. “Keeping incumbents in office is easier than having open seats.”

Letlow’s campaign cropped up after roughly two months of murmurs that she might enter the race. Cassidy was already facing a primary challenge from his right flank, but Letlow’s candidacy likely marks the most existential threat to his Senate future given Trump’s support. 

The endorsement also came shortly after Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) appeared in Louisiana alongside Cassidy for an event to promote the party’s “big, beautiful bill” and a fundraiser. Thune and the Senate GOP campaign arm have maintained their support for the Louisiana senator. 

Cassidy has long been a political target of Trump’s as he voted to convict him for his actions on Jan. 6, 2021. He also has been a vocal opponent of the administration’s health care maneuvers despite originally voting for the confirmation of Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr.

Thune and Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso (R-Wyo.) also appeared at the U.S./Mexico border alongside Cornyn nearly two weeks ago. Cornyn is squaring off with Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton and Rep. Wesley Hunt (R-Texas).

data.title: Resistance and everyday life: Lessons from Warsaw
data.description:
On the last day of January 1991, my family and I, with our dog, Willie, were on a Pan Am flight relocating to Warsaw. Living there would deepen my husband’s commitment as an American advisor to Poland, which had become free of Soviet control and was singularly intent on building the democracy and free enterprise system its people had long dreamed of.  

Until early 2025, if you had asked me my takeaway from my Polish experience, I would have answered that it was deep friendships and the privilege of watching lives change for the good.  

What a difference this last year has brought. Post-Jan. 20, 2025 — the day of President Trump’s second inauguration — I’ve gone back to the questions that I brought with me on that plane. Reflection has brought to the fore a more profound lesson that I never thought might have any applicability to my own comfortable American life.  

In my decades of living in, and then routinely visiting Poland, in those long friendships, I received a gradual education in the truths of living under unjust and disturbing systems, and specifically, what resistance meant to individuals.  

Some friends had living memories of the Nazi occupation; a few had survived the Holocaust. Everyone had muscled through some part of communist control. As a Poland novice, I arrived with many questions, spanning all these periods.  

In the worst case — wartime — how did people live when atrocities were performed on Polish soil, sometimes a street or so away? For the victims of overt violence, why was open revolt rare in the ghettos and death camps when facing certain death anyway? What did it mean to live a normal life in an abnormal environment? How does living a normal life differ from acquiescence?

On a personal level, as someone untested, I asked myself how I might have acted had I lived through any of those periods. Would I have risked my life? Would I have tried to fade into the woodwork?

What I learned, intellectually, the product of numerous conversations as well as spontaneous moments that revealed personal history (a friend offhandedly pointing out the location of his wartime “flying school,” an underground way of defying the ban on educating Polish children) was how ridiculous my question was. You can never know how you will respond to highly challenging and unusual circumstances until the moment when you face them in reality. 

Flash forward to my life in 2025 America. Daily, if not hourly, the current administration restricts freedoms, circumvents democratic processes, violates laws, says absurd and dangerous things. Trump flaunts international norms. 

My most personal encounter with this sledgehammer was the gutting of the Woodrow Wilson International Center for Scholars, where I had been associated since 2011. In a cynical joke on the universe, a Potemkin Village Wilson Center remains.   

Has any of that affected my life? My mood is not good. I regularly attend demonstrations. I send money to groups that litigate on behalf of the victims of this bad policy. I apologize to my international friends and, more poignantly, to a Ukrainian friend in Kyiv.  

And then, like my fellow “dissidents,” I garden, cook dinner, watch movies, see my friends, exercise — pretty much the same life I lived before Jan. 20, 2025. Trump dominates too many dinner party conversations, but the dinner doesn’t lack for food or drink. Meanwhile, Kilmar Abrego Garcia was released from federal detention around Christmas, but his fate as a free man is still working its way through the legal system.  

Now I am beginning to understand the structure and rhythm of my Polish friends’ lives. It isn’t obvious to anyone, even a Pole with hundreds of years of resistance history, how to proceed, and even less obvious what should be your individual responsibility. These conditions are very new for complacent Americans. The most challenging part might be the disconnect between what is happening out there — often to people we don’t know — and the steady pace of what we know as normal.

The normal is what keeps us going, in order to find the resources to do the unusual. Some people dig deep and set the standard for others. Understanding this has made me more optimistic about my own country and its prospects. It is taking us a while to start to get organized. Fear held some people back and still does.  

But, slowly but surely, Americans are breaking past fear. People one neighborhood over from me blow whistles to warn their endangered neighbors when ICE enters their streets. Grandmothers walk unrelated children to school so that their undocumented parents won’t be exposed. 

A former Marine, a woman, created a resource to help state-level officials, governors, state attorneys general and mayors manage aggressive immigration enforcement in their cities. And performers are canceling prized slots at the Kennedy Center — a decision, in the words of dancer Doug Varone, “financially devastating but morally exhilarating.”   

These are small and growing beginnings. Here’s to a year where that trend flourishes. Here’s to the growing American resistance.

Ruth Greenspan Bell was a public policy scholar at the Woodrow Wilson International Center for Scholars from 2011 through 2025.

data.title: Renee Good shot three times, grazed a fourth time: Private autopsy
data.description:
Renee Good, the 37-year-old mother killed by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer in Minneapolis earlier this month, was shot three times and grazed by a fourth bullet, according to results from a private autopsy. 

The law firm representing Good’s family members released the autopsy results on Wednesday. A representative from Romanucci & Blandin said the examination was conducted “by a highly respected and credentialed medical pathologist.”

The report found “three clear gunshot wound paths” to Good’s body. Two of the bullets, one that hit her left forearm and another that hit her right breast, were “not immediately life-threatening,” the examiner stated in the report. 

Another gunshot grazed her body but did not penetrate it. 

One of the gunshots, however, “entered the left side of her head near the temple and exited the right side of her head,” the autopsy found.

Good was fatally shot on Jan. 7 during the Department of Homeland Security’s (DHS) immigration crackdown in the state. Her killing, and another shooting involving a Venezuelan migrant by an ICE officer in the city a week later, sparked waves of intense protests across the state. 

Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey have condemned the actions of Jonathan Ross, the ICE agent who shot and killed Good. 

In a forceful press conference following the incident, Frey urged ICE to leave the city. 

“Somebody is dead. That’s on you,” the mayor said. “And it’s also on you to leave. It’s on you to make sure that further damage, further loss of life and injury, is not done.”

DHS Secretary Kristi Noem and other Trump administration officials has claimed that Good posed a threat to Ross’ life and that the ICE officer was acting in self-defense when he fired.

The private report precedes the results from the Hennepin County Medical Examiner’s Office autopsy of Good’s body. The Hill has reached out to the office for more information on when this report will be made public. 

In a Wednesday statement, the law firm’s lead attorney, Antonio M. Romanucci said the private autopsy is helpful in their effort to building their case. 

“We believe the evidence we are gathering and will continue to gather in our investigation will suffice to prove our case,” the lawyer said. “The video evidence depicting the events of January 7, 2026, is clear, particularly when viewed through the standards of reasonable policing and totality of circumstances.

“Additionally, our legal team will continue its unwavering and proactive advocacy for Renee’s life and family,” he added.

data.title: ICE detains 5-year-old Minnesota boy, father, school says
data.description:
Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers on Tuesday detained a 5-year-old boy and his father in Minnesota after the boy came home from preschool, according to several media reports.

Columbia Heights Public School District Superintendent Zena Stenvik told Minnesota Public Radio (MPR) that Liam Conejo Ramos and his father were approached by masked federal agents in their driveway.

“Another adult living in the home was outside and begged the agents to let him take care of the small child, and was refused,” Stenvik said. “Instead, the agent took the child out of the still-running car, led him to the door and directed him to knock on the door asking to be let in in order to see if anyone else was home, essentially using a 5-year-old as bait.”

The father, Adrian Alexander Conejo Arias, ran from the officers but was quickly detained, the Washington Post reported. The Department of Homeland Security’s (DHS) policy is that ICE will place children in the custody of a safe person designated by the parent.

DHS spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin defended detaining Arias, saying he was from Ecuador and “RELEASED into the U.S. by the Biden administration,” and said officers “did NOT target a child.”

“As agents approached the driver Adrian Alexander Conejo Arias, fled on foot — abandoning his child,” she said in a statement. “For the child’s safety, one of our ICE officers remained with the child while the other officers apprehended Conejo Arias.”

Ramos’ brother, a middle school student, returned home 20 minutes later and only found his mother, the Minnesota Star Tribune reported. Officers took Ramos and his father away and the two are believed to be in a detention facility in Texas.

Stenvik told MPR that Ramos’ family had an “active asylum case” with no orders for deportation, saying she has seen the “legal paperwork with my own eyes.”

“Every step of their immigration process has been doing what they’ve been asked to do, and so this is just … cruelty,” Marc Prokosch, the lawyer representing Ramos’ family, told MPR.

Prokosch noted that detaining Ramos was “probably not” illegal, but just “because something is legal doesn’t mean it’s moral. You know, yes, they may have the legal authority to detain a 5-year-old, but why?”

Ramos is one of four Columbia Heights students detained by ICE this month, the Post reported. Masked immigration enforcement officers detained a 17-year-old high school driving to school earlier Tuesday, with no parents present. Another 17-year-old high school student were also detained last week after ICE officers “pushed their way into an apartment” and detained the student and her mother, the school district told the Post.

Earlier this month, the agency also detained a 10-year-old fourth grade student and her mother, the Post reported, citing the school district. The student called her father and told her ICE officers were taking her to school. The father went to the school and found his daughter and wife were taken to a detention center in Texas.

Local and state leaders in Minnesota also called for a drawback in federal immigration enforcement activities following the death of Renee Good, a Minneapolis woman shot and killed by an ICE officer on Jan. 7. The shooting sparked outrage, prompting Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) to demand that federal agents leave the city.

Outrage grew further after the Trump administration defended the shooting, accusing Good of “domestic terrorism” for allegedly attempting to run over the officers with her SUV. Protests erupted across the country, with a federal judge ordering ICE to stop using retaliation to suppress peaceful protests in Minnesota. On Tuesday, a federal appeals court lifted those restrictions.

The Department of Justice issued subpoenas for Frey and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) on Tuesday, accusing them obstructing an immigration enforcement investigation. Walz called it “partisan distraction,” and Frey said he will continue “doing the job I was elected to do: keeping our community safe and standing up for our values.”

data.title: DHS launches 'Operation Catch of the Day' enforcement action in Maine
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security’s (DHS) newest immigration crackdown, “Operation Catch of the Day,” is underway in Maine. 

“We have launched Operation Catch of the Day to target the worst of the worst criminal illegal aliens in the state,” DHS Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin said in a Wednesday statement. “On the first day of operations, we arrested illegal aliens charged and convicted of aggravated assault, false imprisonment, and endangering the welfare of a child.”

Additionally, McLaughlin criticized the state’s Democratic governor, saying Janet Mills and fellow Maine officials “have made it abundantly clear that they would rather stand with criminal illegal aliens than protect law-abiding American citizens.”

In a video posted on the social platform X prior to DHS’s announcement, Mills addressed speculation that the department was planning to deploy federal authorities to the state. The governor said in the Jan. 14 clip that she has been coordinating with local law enforcement and the state’s attorney general’s office to prepare a response to the operation.

 “If any operations take place, our goal — as always — will be to protect the safety and the rights of the people of Maine,” Mills said. 

Mills went on to criticize Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers’ tactics in previous operations, saying Maine’s law enforcement is “held to high professional standards.”

“I’ll tell you this, they don’t wear a mask to shield their identities, and they don’t arrest people in order to fill a quota,” Mills said. “To the federal government, I say this: If your plan is to come here to be provocative and to undermine the civil rights of Maine residents, do not be confused. Those tactics are not welcome here.”

Following the operation’s announcement, Maine Sen. Susan Collins (R) affirmed DHS’s authority to arrest undocumented immigrants in the state, but she cautioned that these operations should not impact immigrants who are in the country legally.

“Immigrants have long been an important part of our state, and they continue to make valuable contributions to our communities,” Collins said in a Wednesday statement. “People who are in this country legally should not be targets of ICE investigations. They should be able to continue to go to work, school, and other public places and carry on with their normal lives.”

Additionally, the senator urged ICE to provide their officers with body cameras and “de-escalation training.”

“At this time of heightened tensions, these steps could help improve trust, accountability, and safety.”

Collins noted that DHS’s funding bill, which is currently under consideration in Congress, allocates money for these measures. 

The Maine operation follows DHS’s weeks-long advances in Minnesota, which resulted in clashes between residents and federal officers after the fatal shooting of Renee Good by an ICE officer. 

Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) said in a statement this week that families are “scared” and children “are afraid to go to school” because of federal officers’ actions.

The Justice Department has since launched an investigation into five of the state’s officials, including Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D). Walz called the investigation a “partisan distraction” and said his state “will not be drawn into political theater.

Mills earlier this month expressed her support of Mainers’ rights to protest, but she urged them to do so peacefully and “to meet any hostility with reserve and resolve.”

Additionally, the governor echoed the words of Minnesotan officials, who have repeatedly lambasted the Trump administration for using tactics they allege are meant to unnerve their constituents and state officials. 

“Maine will not be intimidated,” Mills said. “We will not betray the values that make us who we are.”
